content:
Member-only story

Exploring “vibe coding,” AI-assisted productivity, and when deep expertise matters.

15 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Executive Summary: Artificial intelligence is transforming how software is written — but do these advances make traditional programming languages merely tools, or do they increase the need for solid coding skills? In this article we define vibe coding (AI-first, prompt-driven development), trace its origin (Andrej Karpathy’s 2025 tweet) and its evolution into “agentic engineering.” We review recent research (2024–2026) from academia and industry on AI-assisted coding: for example, surveys and studies (GitHub, StackOverflow, DORA, Anthropic, METR, CSA) show mixed productivity gains, often outweighed by added complexity or risk. We examine empirical findings on developer speed, learning, and error rates with AI tools (e.g. one study found developers working with Copilot felt faster but were 19% slower on tasks; another found 86% of developers satisfied yet most saved <1 hour/week). We survey the risks of vibe coding — hallucinated APIs, race conditions, security holes (45% of AI code had OWASP-level vulnerabilities) — versus more supervised AI engineering. We provide practical guidance: when limited oversight (“full vibe”) may be OK (quick prototypes, internal tools) and when it isn’t…
